cf_parsed.subtask_body:
I used the extra pass to test one different global shape rather than tiny local swaps: put 1 next to 8 and send 12 into the lower-right chain, giving
0-15-3-2-13-17-8-1-12-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4-0 and close variants.
These are worse: 8-1 is ~36 and especially 12-16 is ~34, so they lose against 8-12-1-16.
I also checked whether 17 should connect to 16 before 8, i.e. ...13-17-16-1-12-8..., but 12-8 is good while 8 would then need a bad successor; net worse.
Another plausible shape is to move 5 between 7 and11, or use ...9-5-11-19..., but that increases one of 9-5, 5-19, or 7-11 too much.
A more radical top-left variant 0-4-18-7-5-11-19-9-10-14-6-16-1-12-8-17-13-2-3-15-0 is roughly comparable by perimeter logic, but the wrap edges 15-0 and 4-0 plus 2-3-15 do not obviously beat the incumbent and likely worsen it.
So after this final comparison, the incumbent still appears best among the hand-checked tours.
cf_parsed.updated_plan_state: Incumbent remains 0-15-3-2-13-17-8-12-1-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4 with estimated length about 435. Extra global-shape checks did not reveal a better valid tour, so stopping remains preferred.

parsed.subtask_body:
I tested the most plausible wraparound and top-middle alternatives against the incumbent
0-15-3-2-13-17-8-12-1-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4-0.
Key checks:
1) Replace closing edges 18-4 and 4-0 by 18-0 and 4-15 (equivalently reversing the first block): much worse, because 18-0 and 4-15 are both long.
2) Reconnect around 0,15,3,4 using 0-4-18-...-15: also worse; 0-4 is fine already but 18-15 or 4-15 inflates too much.
3) Swap the top-middle order from ...3-2-13-17... to ...3-13-2-17...: worse, since 3-13 is short but 2-17 is very long.
4) Test ...2-17-13... instead of ...2-13-17...: clearly worse because 2-17 + 13-8 style continuation dominates.
5) Try routing 18 to 13 instead of 4, e.g. ...7-18-13-2... or ...7-18-13-4...: both create either a long 18-13 edge plus a bad return, or force 4 elsewhere with no net gain.
6) Try moving 4 next to 0 on the other side, such as ...18-7... and ...4-0-15... variants: no improvement; the current 18-4 and 4-0 are both already reasonable.
7) Lower-left chain rechecks like ...10-19-9-... and ...11-7-5... remained inferior.
Conclusion: no checked local 2-opt/relocation beat the incumbent. The current tour looks stable and likely near locally optimal by hand.
parsed.updated_plan_state: Incumbent remains 0-15-3-2-13-17-8-12-1-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4 with estimated length about 435. No obvious improving local move remains from manual inspection; stopping is likely better than spending more time on low-probability tweaks.

transcript.content:
PLAN_STATE: Start from geometric clusters and the baseline to build a noncrossing perimeter-style cycle.
Use rough left-to-right layering: top chain {0,15,3,2}, middle {18,4,13,17,16}, bottom/right {8,12,1,6,14,19,10,9}, with 5/7/11 linking left side.
First subtask: estimate a strong candidate by hand and compare a few orderings around the right cluster {1,8,12,17,16,13,3,2}.
Prioritize removing obvious long edges/crossings from the baseline, especially around 1-12-8 and left-side insertion of 5/9/10.
Then do local 2-opt style reasoning on the best candidate: swap adjacent subsequences if two medium edges can replace one long edge.
Keep tour valid and simple; if a candidate seems clearly below baseline, refine rather than exploring many unrelated starts.
Later subtask, if needed: test alternative placement of city 0 relative to {15,3,2} and city 5 relative to {9,11,7}.
Stop once I have a clean feasible tour with no obvious crossing and only short regional links plus two reasonable wraparound edges.
NEXT_SUB: {"id": 1, "desc": "Construct and evaluate one strong noncrossing tour candidate, focusing on right-cluster ordering and left-side insertion choices", "p_solve": 0.45, "time_budget_s": 180}

SUB_1: Built a perimeter-style candidate and compared a few right-cluster orderings by rough Euclidean sums.
A strong noncrossing tour is:
0-15-2-3-13-17-8-12-1-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4-(back to 0).
This uses natural local links on top/right/bottom/left and avoids the baseline’s awkward 1-12-8 handling.
Approx edge lengths for this candidate:
0-15 17, 15-2 48, 2-3 22, 3-13 14, 13-17 30, 17-8 19, 8-12 6, 12-1 32, 1-16 34, 16-6 24,
6-14 12, 14-10 13, 10-9 18, 9-19 19, 19-11 11, 11-5 18, 5-7 20, 7-18 29, 18-4 8, 4-0 43.
Total is about 438, clearly beating baseline 481.
I also considered swapping 1 and 16, but 12-16 plus 1-6 was worse than 12-1 plus 16-6.
Left-side ordering 9-5-11 or 5-9-19 created longer links than 9-19-11-5.
One possible remaining weakness is the long 15-2 edge; alternative 15-3-2 would force a worse connection elsewhere.
Another possible tweak is near the lower-left chain {9,19,11,5,7}, but current order is already quite coherent.
So this candidate looks like a solid incumbent with no obvious crossing and only a few moderately long connector edges.
BEST_GUESS: {"tour":[0,15,2,3,13,17,8,12,1,16,6,14,10,9,19,11,5,7,18,4]}
UPDATED_PLAN_STATE: Keep the current strong feasible tour. Spend one more subtask checking the two most promising local improvements: relocate city 1 relative to {12,8,16,6} and test alternative top-left/right stitching around {0,15,2,3,4,18}. Preserve a valid answer every turn.
QUALITY_FORECAST: {"p_gap_le_2":0.08,"p_gap_le_5":0.32,"p_gap_le_10":0.72}
CONTINUE_FORECAST: {"p_improve":0.42,"expected_delta_score":1.6,"expected_gap_reduction":3.0}
DECISION: continue
NEXT_SUB: {"id":2,"desc":"Try a few high-value 2-opt/relocation changes around edges (15,2), (12,1), (1,16), and the lower-left chain to see if the ~438 tour can be shortened further","p_solve":0.42,"time_budget_s":180}

SUB_2: I checked the highest-yield local alternatives around the incumbent tour 0-15-2-3-13-17-8-12-1-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4-0.
Main finding: the edge pair 15-2 and 4-0 is weak. Reconnecting the top-left as 0-15-3-2 and attaching 4 to 13 instead of 0 gives a clear gain.
Candidate improvement:
0-15-3-2-17-8-12-1-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4-13-0.
Approx lengths:
0-15 17, 15-3 30, 3-2 22, 2-17 50, 17-8 19, 8-12 6, 12-1 32, 1-16 34, 16-6 24, 6-14 12,
14-10 13, 10-9 18, 9-19 19, 19-11 11, 11-5 18, 5-7 20, 7-18 29, 18-4 8, 4-13 20, 13-0 52.
This sums to about 455, so despite fixing 15-2 it is worse because 2-17 and 13-0 are too long.
Better variant found:
0-15-3-2-13-17-8-12-1-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4-0.
Compared to incumbent, this replaces 15-2 + 2-3 + 3-13 with 15-3 + 3-2 + 2-13.
Approx change: (30+22+29) - (48+22+14) = -3, so a small improvement to about 435.
I also checked moving 1 after 6 or after 8; both worsen because 12-16 or 6-12 becomes too long, while 1-16 is already reasonable.
Swapping the lower-left order to ...10-19-9... or ...11-7-5... also worsened due to longer local edges.
So the best tour from this pass is the simple top reorder:
0-15-3-2-13-17-8-12-1-16-6-14-10-9-19-11-5-7-18-4-0, roughly 435.
BEST_GUESS: {"tour":[0,15,3,2,13,17,8,12,1,16,6,14,10,9,19,11,5,7,18,4]}
UPDATED_PLAN_STATE: Current incumbent is the reordered top chain with estimated length about 435. One more subtask should test only a few plausible 2-opt moves involving the wrap edges (4,0), (0,15), and the central link (2,13), plus whether 18 should connect to 13 instead of 4. Keep the current valid tour unless a clearly shorter noncrossing alternative appears.
QUALITY_FORECAST: {"p_gap_le_2":0.12,"p_gap_le_5":0.4,"p_gap_le_10":0.8}
CONTINUE_FORECAST: {"p_improve":0.28,"expected_delta_score":0.9,"expected_gap_reduction":1.7}
DECISION: continue
NEXT_SUB: {"id":3,"desc":"Do one final focused pass on wraparound 2-opt moves involving edges near 0,4,15 and the top-middle connection around 2 and 13","p_solve":0.28,"time_budget_s":120}

4) Test ...2-17-13... instead of ...2-13-17...: clearly worse because 2-17 + 13-8 style continuation dominates.
5) Try routing 18 to 13 instead of 4, e.g. ...7-18-13-2... or ...7-18-13-4...: both create either a long 18-13 edge plus a bad return, or force 4 elsewhere with no net gain.
